Mt. Mendori (Mt. Amaoka) is in the background. }}]] '''Kuwayama Shrine'' (Kuwayama Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}Kamiyada Town. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|Old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Fusha}}. It is also commonly known as ``Yada-sha'' or ``Yada-miya''. === Overview === It is enshrined at the eastern foot of Mt. Menkoyama in the southern part of {{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}. Many ancient ruins remain in the surrounding area, including Mt. In the {{jalink|Kameoka Basin|Q11181997}}, which is said to have been a lake in ancient times, the enshrined deity {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Oomi Takamikoto (Ōkuninushi)}} This shrine is based on the legend that, as part of the efforts to build the country, {{jalink|Hozu Gorge|Q10887680}} was excavated to create a basin, and the hoes used for the excavation were piled up here. Within the fence of the precincts, Hachiman Shrine is built alongside the main shrine in the same size and style, and both shrine buildings are registered as {{jalink|List of Kyoto Prefecture Designated and Registered Cultural Properties|Q11375201|Kyoto Prefecture Registered Cultural Properties}}. Many autumn leaves are planted in the precincts, and it is also a famous spot for autumn leaves. In addition, the annual festival ``{{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}'' is a traditional festival in Kameoka City, where people carry the gods to Kameoka City. === Enshrined God === * Kuwayama Shrine: '''{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōmi Takamikoto}}''' (Onamuchi no Mikoto) *: Okuninushi is the same as {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} and is an Izumo god. It is said to be the main deity that has been enshrined since its founding. * Hachiman Shrine: '''{{jalink|Honda Beszon}}''' (Hondawake no Mikoto) *: Honda Besson is the same as {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}}, and {{jalink|Hachimanjin|Q261637}}. It is said to be a guest deity that has been enshrined since the first year of {{jalink|Eiman|Q1193868}} ({{jalink|1165|Q19676}}). The divine crest of Kuwayama Shrine is a rabbit, in honor of Okuninushi no Mikoto, and that of Hachiman Shrine is a dove, as is customary. === History === === Legend === [[File:Hozukyō (Kameoka).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Entrance to the traditional site of {{jalink|Hozukyo|Q10887680}}}}{{small|{{jalink|Ukeda Shrine|Q11631810}} on the left bank, {{jalink|Kuwata Shrine #Kuwata Shrine (Yamamoto, Shinomachi, Kameoka City)|Q11537594|Kuwata Shrine}} is enshrined there. }}]] ==== Founding of Kuwasan Shrine ==== Regarding the founding of Kuwayama Shrine, there is a legend in the "Yada no Saiki" written in the first year of {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}). . According to this, in ancient times, Izumo Okami and Yagami colluded with Mt. Kurogara to develop the {{jalink|Kameoka Basin|Q11181997}}, which was a mud lake. He then rides an oak boat and cuts open the Uketa Gorge (currently the mouth of {{jalink|Hozu Gorge|Q10887680}}), and succeeds in draining the water in the direction of {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}, thus creating a vast plain. Izumo Okami was enshrined at the foot of Mt. Amaoka and was revered as Kuwayama Daimyojin. Additionally, this god did not attend the October (Kannazuki) meeting in Izumo Province, but the eight gods in the county met at Kuwayama Shrine . It is also said that the name of the shrine was given to Kuwayama because the hoes used for excavation were piled up there. (Also see "{{jalink|Kameoka Basin #Lake Lore|Q11181997}}"). ==== Hachimangu Shrine's solicitation ==== [[File:Kuwayama-jinja koike.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|A small pond between Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine}}{{small|It is said that it was established so that the two shrines would not fight each other. }}]] Regarding the parallel Hachiman Shrine, it is said in "Yada no Saiki" that it descended to Mt. Menfuri (Mt. Amaoka) in the first year of {{jalink|Eiman|Q1193868}} ({{jalink|1165|Q19676}}). Since then, it has been enshrined as Shin Hachimangu Shrine next to Kuwayamagu Shrine, but there were thunderstorms and fights between pigeons and rabbits. The villagers thought this was a discord between the two gods, and it is said that the conflict was resolved by moving Hachimangu Shrine to Sugitani . Although it is unclear whether this is related to the legend, Kuwayama Shrine currently uses a rabbit, and Hachiman Shrine uses a pigeon. Even after both shrines were moved to their current location in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}14 ({{jalink|1609|Q6762}}), a small pond was built between the two shrines to prevent conflicts, and the pond still exists today. === Outline === According to the shrine's legend, Kuwazangu Shrine was founded in the 2nd year of Wado ({{jalink|709|Q23514}}), and was originally located in Iou Valley, behind Mt. There are small shrines dotted all the way to the top of Mt. Menduri, and at the top of the mountain there is a shadow stone that is said to have descended from Hachimangu Shrine . There are many ancient tombs in the surrounding area, and there is also a theory that there was a kokuga or a county seat based on the legend that a {{jalink|tunkura|Q11465761}} was established in the Miyake area, suggesting that Mt. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it is written as ``{{jalink|Tamba Province|Q954511}}{{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}} Kuwayama Shrine,'' and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}. It is said that the physician {{jalink|Yasuyori Tamba|Q1466794}}, who was born in Iōdani, believed in Kuwayama Shrine, and the name ``Iōdani'' also came from Yasuyori, who was well versed in medicine.The place name ``Yada'' is related to Kuwayama Shrine, and there are 8 shrines: Otabisho, Rakuta, Yuuda, Hanada, Yokata, Sumoda, Babada, Zoyoda, and Hoshada. The shrine was called ``Hatta'' because it had , and later changed to ``Yada'' when {{jalink|Minamoto no Yorimasa|Q164948}} received the shrine. . It is also said that festivals such as the mikoshi festival, eight-day festival, niwa kagura, horse racing, sumo wrestling, sarugaku, etc. have been held since ancient times. name=chimei/> invaded Tanba in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}4 ({{jalink|1576|Q6700}}) nk|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} abolished those festivals, and Daichi-in (currently an abandoned temple) was built as {{jalink|Bettoji|Q11396755}}. Eight Kanda were also requisitioned . {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}14 ({{jalink|1609|Q6762}}), {{jalink|Kameyama Castle (Tamba Province)|Q1037429|Kameyama Castle}} The lord {{jalink|Mori Okanabe|Q11473887}} built a new shrine building at the current location and moved it, and also donated the shrine land . In {{jalink|Kanei|Q1192913}}16 ({{jalink|1639|Q6897}}), the domain of the shrine was donated by Sadafusa Suganuma, the lord of the domain .In {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} 9th year ({{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), Morichika Sugihara wrote ``Festival Chukoki'' and revived the festival . In the same year, a mikoshi was donated by the castle lord {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}}, and since then the annual festival has become popular as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kuchitamba. {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} in 1998, and was promoted to {{jalink|Fusha}} in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}3. === Precincts === [[File:Kuwayama-jinja kuwayamagū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Kuwayamamiya}}]] [[File:Kuwayama-jinja hachimangū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Hachimangu Shrine}}]] The main shrine, Kuwayamagu Shrine, and Hachimangu Shrine are located within the same fence, with Kuwayamagu Shrine on the left and Hachimangu Shrine on the right. Both shrines have the same format and size, and were built in the same year as {{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}11 ({{jalink|1814|Q6943}}) of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}. Kuwayama Shrine's {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}} exists since {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}}3 ({{jalink|1462|Q6546}}). The style is Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} and has {{jalink|staggered gables}}. A one-room {{jalink|kara gable|Q977293}} shrine is attached to the front, and the roof is {{jalink|persimmon thatched}}. Both shrines are registered as Kyoto Prefecture's registered cultural properties. [http://spogaku.pref.kyoto.lg.jp/asset/1280.html Kuwayama Shrine Precinct Shrine Hachiman Shrine Main Hall] (Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Based on the legend that Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine were not on good terms, a small pond was built between the two shrines. File:Kuwayama-jinja haiden-2.jpg|Handiden (shared with Ninomiya) File:Kuwayama-jinja torii-2.jpg|Torii in the precincts File:Kuwayama-jinja teien.jpg|Garden === Setsusha === '''Precinct shrine''' * Kanayama Shrine * Kashifune Shrine - Enshrines the same deity as {{jalink|Kashifune Shrine|Q11543587}} in {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Takatsuki City|Q335423}}. * Takagi Shrine * Hiyoshi Shrine * Kumano Shrine * Inari Hipo Shrine * Atago Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime}}. It is enshrined on a small island in the pond. * Hyakudayu Shrine - Deities enshrined: Toyowama Tokami, Kushiwama Tokami. is located at the entrance to the shrine grounds and is said to be the first shrine you should visit before visiting the head office. '''{{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}''' * Katahara Shrine (Yokomachi, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|39.21|N|135|34|51.70|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Katahara Shrine (Otabisho)}}) *: Enshrined in the ruins of a mansion south of Kameyama Castle in the center of Kameoka. Katahara Shrine is a shrine dedicated to the first lord of {{jalink|Tamba Kameyama Clan|Q10854981|Kameyama Clan}}, {{jalink|Nobuaki Matsudaira|Q8515964}}, and was founded in 1898. Katahara Shrine] (Tamba Shrine [Personal Site]). . During the annual festival (Kameoka Festival), a mikoshi (portable shrine) is passed to the shrine as a traveling place.* Former Otabisho (Ichi-no-Torii) (Nakayada-cho, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|09.39|N|135|34|55.48|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Ichi-no-Torii (Old Otabisho)}}) *: This is where the Otabisho used to be, and currently there is a stone torii (Ichi-no-Torii) built by {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}} in the first year of {{jalink|Joo|Q1145081}} ({{jalink|1652|Q6962}}). === Festival === === Annual festival === * {{jalink|Saidan Festival|Q11546824}} ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * {{jalink|Setsubun Festival}} ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Flower Festival ({{jalink|April 15th|Q2497}}) * Eight Day Festival ({{jalink|May 8th|Q2555}}) * Minazuki Festival ({{jalink|July 2nd|Q2697}}) * Annual Festival (Autumn Festival, {{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}) ({{jalink|October 1st|Q2913}}-{{jalink|October 26th|Q2953|26th}}) ** {{jalink|Shinko Festival|Q123907414}} ({{jalink|October 20th|Q2959}}) ** Yoyoyama ({{jalink|October 23rd|Q2958}}) ** Yoimiya ({{jalink|October 24th|Q2955}}) ** Main Festival ({{jalink|October 25th|Q2954}}) * Fire Festival ({{jalink|December 3rd|Q2298}}) === Kameoka Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} held in October is called ``''{{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}'''. In ancient times, {{jalink|September 25th (lunar calendar)|Q840119|September 25th}} was the Shinko Festival, and {{jalink|September 30th (lunar calendar)|Q840189|30th}} was the Kanko Festival. It is said that the festival at Kuwayama Shrine was abolished by {{jalink|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} who invaded Tamba, but {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735 }}In 1681|Q7645}}, a mikoshi was donated by the lord of Kameyama Castle, {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}}, and since then, the annual festival has been known as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kouchi-Tamba, and based on tradition, oak boats have also been paraded around the festival [http://www.k-yamahoko.com/history.html History of Yamahoko Events] ([http://www.k-yamahoko.com/index.html Kameoka Festival Homepage]) . At the festival, 11 floats are paraded like the {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}} at {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q692714}} in Kyoto, and mikoshi from both Kuwayama and Hachimangu Shrines are carried to Katahara Shrine, the Otabisho. === Cultural assets === === Kyoto prefecture registered cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Kuwayama Shrine and the Hachiman Shrine main hall (structure) - Registered on April 15, 1983 (Showa 58). * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Registered on March 19, 2004. === Kyoto Prefecture Cultural Properties Environmental Conservation District === * Kuwayama Shrine Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District - Decided on May 15, 1985. === Cultural property designated by Kameoka City === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Designated on August 3, 1998. === Others === * Kameoka's top 100 natural attractions: Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}Kamiyada-cho Kamigakiuchi 22 '''Access''' * From {{jalink|West Japan Railway|Q502125}} (JR West) {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Kameoka Station|Q1043696}} **Walk: about 30 minutes ** Bus: Take the Kameoka City Community Bus (East Course) and get off at the "Kuwayama Jinja-mae" bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Nasu Yoichido === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === {{small|(References used in writing the article)}} '''Historical materials''' * “Tanba Province Kuwata District Yadano Shrine” *: Selected by Morichika Sugihara of Kameyama Gocho ({{jalink|Machi Toshiyori|Q11578236}}) in the first year of {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}). Mt. Mendori (Mt. Amaoka) is in the background. }}]] '''Kuwayama Shrine'' (Kuwayama Shrine) is located in {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}Kamiyada Town. In {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|Old shrine rank}} is {{jalink|Fusha}}. It is also commonly known as ``Yada-sha'' or ``Yada-miya''. === Overview === It is enshrined at the eastern foot of Mt. Menkoyama in the southern part of {{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}. Many ancient ruins remain in the surrounding area, including Mt. In the {{jalink|Kameoka Basin|Q11181997}}, which is said to have been a lake in ancient times, the enshrined deity {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Oomi Takamikoto (Ōkuninushi)}} This shrine is based on the legend that, as part of the efforts to build the country, {{jalink|Hozu Gorge|Q10887680}} was excavated to create a basin, and the hoes used for the excavation were piled up here. Within the fence of the precincts, Hachiman Shrine is built alongside the main shrine in the same size and style, and both shrine buildings are registered as {{jalink|List of Kyoto Prefecture Designated and Registered Cultural Properties|Q11375201|Kyoto Prefecture Registered Cultural Properties}}. Many autumn leaves are planted in the precincts, and it is also a famous spot for autumn leaves. In addition, the annual festival ``{{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}'' is a traditional festival in Kameoka City, where people carry the gods to Kameoka City. === Enshrined God === * Kuwayama Shrine: '''{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōmi Takamikoto}}''' (Onamuchi no Mikoto) *: Okuninushi is the same as {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} and is an Izumo god. It is said to be the main deity that has been enshrined since its founding. * Hachiman Shrine: '''{{jalink|Honda Beszon}}''' (Hondawake no Mikoto) *: Honda Besson is the same as {{jalink|Emperor Ojin|Q317997}}, and {{jalink|Hachimanjin|Q261637}}. It is said to be a guest deity that has been enshrined since the first year of {{jalink|Eiman|Q1193868}} ({{jalink|1165|Q19676}}). The divine crest of Kuwayama Shrine is a rabbit, in honor of Okuninushi no Mikoto, and that of Hachiman Shrine is a dove, as is customary. === History === === Legend === [[File:Hozukyō (Kameoka).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Entrance to the traditional site of {{jalink|Hozukyo|Q10887680}}}}{{small|{{jalink|Ukeda Shrine|Q11631810}} on the left bank, {{jalink|Kuwata Shrine #Kuwata Shrine (Yamamoto, Shinomachi, Kameoka City)|Q11537594|Kuwata Shrine}} is enshrined there. }}]] ==== Founding of Kuwasan Shrine ==== Regarding the founding of Kuwayama Shrine, there is a legend in the "Yada no Saiki" written in the first year of {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}). . According to this, in ancient times, Izumo Okami and Yagami colluded with Mt. Kurogara to develop the {{jalink|Kameoka Basin|Q11181997}}, which was a mud lake. He then rides an oak boat and cuts open the Uketa Gorge (currently the mouth of {{jalink|Hozu Gorge|Q10887680}}), and succeeds in draining the water in the direction of {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}, thus creating a vast plain. Izumo Okami was enshrined at the foot of Mt. Amaoka and was revered as Kuwayama Daimyojin. Additionally, this god did not attend the October (Kannazuki) meeting in Izumo Province, but the eight gods in the county met at Kuwayama Shrine . It is also said that the name of the shrine was given to Kuwayama because the hoes used for excavation were piled up there. (Also see "{{jalink|Kameoka Basin #Lake Lore|Q11181997}}"). ==== Hachimangu Shrine's solicitation ==== [[File:Kuwayama-jinja koike.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|A small pond between Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine}}{{small|It is said that it was established so that the two shrines would not fight each other. }}]] Regarding the parallel Hachiman Shrine, it is said in "Yada no Saiki" that it descended to Mt. Menfuri (Mt. Amaoka) in the first year of {{jalink|Eiman|Q1193868}} ({{jalink|1165|Q19676}}). Since then, it has been enshrined as Shin Hachimangu Shrine next to Kuwayamagu Shrine, but there were thunderstorms and fights between pigeons and rabbits. The villagers thought this was a discord between the two gods, and it is said that the conflict was resolved by moving Hachimangu Shrine to Sugitani . Although it is unclear whether this is related to the legend, Kuwayama Shrine currently uses a rabbit, and Hachiman Shrine uses a pigeon. Even after both shrines were moved to their current location in {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}14 ({{jalink|1609|Q6762}}), a small pond was built between the two shrines to prevent conflicts, and the pond still exists today. === Outline === According to the shrine's legend, Kuwazangu Shrine was founded in the 2nd year of Wado ({{jalink|709|Q23514}}), and was originally located in Iou Valley, behind Mt. There are small shrines dotted all the way to the top of Mt. Menduri, and at the top of the mountain there is a shadow stone that is said to have descended from Hachimangu Shrine . There are many ancient tombs in the surrounding area, and there is also a theory that there was a kokuga or a county seat based on the legend that a {{jalink|tunkura|Q11465761}} was established in the Miyake area, suggesting that Mt. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}} from the middle of the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, it is written as ``{{jalink|Tamba Province|Q954511}}{{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}} Kuwayama Shrine,'' and it is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi Shrine}}. It is said that the physician {{jalink|Yasuyori Tamba|Q1466794}}, who was born in Iōdani, believed in Kuwayama Shrine, and the name ``Iōdani'' also came from Yasuyori, who was well versed in medicine.The place name ``Yada'' is related to Kuwayama Shrine, and there are 8 shrines: Otabisho, Rakuta, Yuuda, Hanada, Yokata, Sumoda, Babada, Zoyoda, and Hoshada. The shrine was called ``Hatta'' because it had , and later changed to ``Yada'' when {{jalink|Minamoto no Yorimasa|Q164948}} received the shrine. . It is also said that festivals such as the mikoshi festival, eight-day festival, niwa kagura, horse racing, sumo wrestling, sarugaku, etc. have been held since ancient times. name=chimei/> invaded Tanba in {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058}}4 ({{jalink|1576|Q6700}}) nk|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} abolished those festivals, and Daichi-in (currently an abandoned temple) was built as {{jalink|Bettoji|Q11396755}}. Eight Kanda were also requisitioned . {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}14 ({{jalink|1609|Q6762}}), {{jalink|Kameyama Castle (Tamba Province)|Q1037429|Kameyama Castle}} The lord {{jalink|Mori Okanabe|Q11473887}} built a new shrine building at the current location and moved it, and also donated the shrine land . In {{jalink|Kanei|Q1192913}}16 ({{jalink|1639|Q6897}}), the domain of the shrine was donated by Sadafusa Suganuma, the lord of the domain .In {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} 9th year ({{jalink|1681|Q7645}}), Morichika Sugihara wrote ``Festival Chukoki'' and revived the festival . In the same year, a mikoshi was donated by the castle lord {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}}, and since then the annual festival has become popular as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kuchitamba. {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) {{jalink|Modern company rank system|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} in 1998, and was promoted to {{jalink|Fusha}} in {{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}3. === Precincts === [[File:Kuwayama-jinja kuwayamagū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Kuwayamamiya}}]] [[File:Kuwayama-jinja hachimangū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Hachimangu Shrine}}]] The main shrine, Kuwayamagu Shrine, and Hachimangu Shrine are located within the same fence, with Kuwayamagu Shrine on the left and Hachimangu Shrine on the right. Both shrines have the same format and size, and were built in the same year as {{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}11 ({{jalink|1814|Q6943}}) of the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}. Kuwayama Shrine's {{jalink|ridge tag|Q8008834}} exists since {{jalink|Kansho|Q1143928}}3 ({{jalink|1462|Q6546}}). The style is Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}} and has {{jalink|staggered gables}}. A one-room {{jalink|kara gable|Q977293}} shrine is attached to the front, and the roof is {{jalink|persimmon thatched}}. Both shrines are registered as Kyoto Prefecture's registered cultural properties. [http://spogaku.pref.kyoto.lg.jp/asset/1280.html Kuwayama Shrine Precinct Shrine Hachiman Shrine Main Hall] (Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Based on the legend that Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine were not on good terms, a small pond was built between the two shrines. File:Kuwayama-jinja haiden-2.jpg|Handiden (shared with Ninomiya) File:Kuwayama-jinja torii-2.jpg|Torii in the precincts File:Kuwayama-jinja teien.jpg|Garden === Setsusha === '''Precinct shrine''' * Kanayama Shrine * Kashifune Shrine - Enshrines the same deity as {{jalink|Kashifune Shrine|Q11543587}} in {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Takatsuki City|Q335423}}. * Takagi Shrine * Hiyoshi Shrine * Kumano Shrine * Inari Hipo Shrine * Atago Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime}}. It is enshrined on a small island in the pond. * Hyakudayu Shrine - Deities enshrined: Toyowama Tokami, Kushiwama Tokami. is located at the entrance to the shrine grounds and is said to be the first shrine you should visit before visiting the head office. '''{{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}''' * Katahara Shrine (Yokomachi, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|39.21|N|135|34|51.70|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Katahara Shrine (Otabisho)}}) *: Enshrined in the ruins of a mansion south of Kameyama Castle in the center of Kameoka. Katahara Shrine is a shrine dedicated to the first lord of {{jalink|Tamba Kameyama Clan|Q10854981|Kameyama Clan}}, {{jalink|Nobuaki Matsudaira|Q8515964}}, and was founded in 1898. Katahara Shrine] (Tamba Shrine [Personal Site]). . During the annual festival (Kameoka Festival), a mikoshi (portable shrine) is passed to the shrine as a traveling place.* Former Otabisho (Ichi-no-Torii) (Nakayada-cho, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|09.39|N|135|34|55.48|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Ichi-no-Torii (Old Otabisho)}}) *: This is where the Otabisho used to be, and currently there is a stone torii (Ichi-no-Torii) built by {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}} in the first year of {{jalink|Joo|Q1145081}} ({{jalink|1652|Q6962}}). === Festival === === Annual festival === * {{jalink|Saidan Festival|Q11546824}} ({{jalink|January 1st|Q2150}}) * {{jalink|Setsubun Festival}} ({{jalink|February 3rd|Q2315}}) * Flower Festival ({{jalink|April 15th|Q2497}}) * Eight Day Festival ({{jalink|May 8th|Q2555}}) * Minazuki Festival ({{jalink|July 2nd|Q2697}}) * Annual Festival (Autumn Festival, {{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}) ({{jalink|October 1st|Q2913}}-{{jalink|October 26th|Q2953|26th}}) ** {{jalink|Shinko Festival|Q123907414}} ({{jalink|October 20th|Q2959}}) ** Yoyoyama ({{jalink|October 23rd|Q2958}}) ** Yoimiya ({{jalink|October 24th|Q2955}}) ** Main Festival ({{jalink|October 25th|Q2954}}) * Fire Festival ({{jalink|December 3rd|Q2298}}) === Kameoka Festival === The {{jalink|regular festival|Q11385469}} held in October is called ``''{{jalink|Kameoka Festival|Q11370928}}'''. In ancient times, {{jalink|September 25th (lunar calendar)|Q840119|September 25th}} was the Shinko Festival, and {{jalink|September 30th (lunar calendar)|Q840189|30th}} was the Kanko Festival. It is said that the festival at Kuwayama Shrine was abolished by {{jalink|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} who invaded Tamba, but {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735 }}In 1681|Q7645}}, a mikoshi was donated by the lord of Kameyama Castle, {{jalink|Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865}}, and since then, the annual festival has been known as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kouchi-Tamba, and based on tradition, oak boats have also been paraded around the festival [http://www.k-yamahoko.com/history.html History of Yamahoko Events] ([http://www.k-yamahoko.com/index.html Kameoka Festival Homepage]) . At the festival, 11 floats are paraded like the {{jalink|Gion Festival|Q979873}} at {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q692714}} in Kyoto, and mikoshi from both Kuwayama and Hachimangu Shrines are carried to Katahara Shrine, the Otabisho. === Cultural assets === === Kyoto prefecture registered cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Kuwayama Shrine and the Hachiman Shrine main hall (structure) - Registered on April 15, 1983 (Showa 58). * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Registered on March 19, 2004. === Kyoto Prefecture Cultural Properties Environmental Conservation District === * Kuwayama Shrine Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District - Decided on May 15, 1985. === Cultural property designated by Kameoka City === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Designated on August 3, 1998. === Others === * Kameoka's top 100 natural attractions: Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka City|Q633996}}Kamiyada-cho Kamigakiuchi 22 '''Access''' * From {{jalink|West Japan Railway|Q502125}} (JR West) {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Kameoka Station|Q1043696}} **Walk: about 30 minutes ** Bus: Take the Kameoka City Community Bus (East Course) and get off at the "Kuwayama Jinja-mae" bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Nasu Yoichido === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === {{small|(References used in writing the article)}} '''Historical materials''' * “Tanba Province Kuwata District Yadano Shrine” *: Selected by Morichika Sugihara of Kameyama Gocho ({{jalink|Machi Toshiyori|Q11578236}}) in the first year of {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} ({{jalink|1673|Q7617}}). Contains folklore about ``Yada no Shrine,'' or Kuwayama Shrine. Mt. Mendori (Mt. Amaoka) is in the background. }}]] '''Kuwayama Shrine'' (Kuwayama Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada Town. In {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill||lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}}. It is also commonly known as ``Yada-sha'' or ``Yada-miya''. === Overview === It is enshrined at the eastern foot of Mt. Menkoyama in the southern part of {{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}. Many ancient ruins remain in the surrounding area, including Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which is said to have been a lake in ancient times, the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Oomi Takamikoto (Ōkuninushi)}} This shrine is based on the legend that, as part of the efforts to build the country, {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}} was excavated to create a basin, and the hoes used for the excavation were piled up here. Within the fence of the precincts, Hachiman Shrine is built alongside the main shrine in the same size and style, and both shrine buildings are registered as {{ill|lt=List of Kyoto Prefecture Designated and Registered Cultural Properties|Q11375201|WD=Q11375201|lt=Kyoto Prefecture Registered Cultural Properties}}. Many autumn leaves are planted in the precincts, and it is also a famous spot for autumn leaves. In addition, the annual festival ``{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'' is a traditional festival in Kameoka City, where people carry the gods to Kameoka City. === Enshrined God === * Kuwayama Shrine: '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōmi Takamikoto}}''' (Onamuchi no Mikoto) *: Okuninushi is the same as {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} and is an Izumo god. It is said to be the main deity that has been enshrined since its founding. * Hachiman Shrine: '''{{ill||lt=Honda Beszon|UNKNOWN}}''' (Hondawake no Mikoto) *: Honda Besson is the same as {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, and {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}. It is said to be a guest deity that has been enshrined since the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). The divine crest of Kuwayama Shrine is a rabbit, in honor of Okuninushi no Mikoto, and that of Hachiman Shrine is a dove, as is customary. === History === === Legend === [[File:Hozukyō (Kameoka).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Entrance to the traditional site of {{ill|lt=Hozukyo|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Ukeda Shrine|Q11631810|WD=Q11631810}} on the left bank, {{ill|lt=Kuwata Shrine #Kuwata Shrine (Yamamoto, Shinomachi, Kameoka City)|Q11537594|WD=Q11537594|lt=Kuwata Shrine}} is enshrined there. }}]] ==== Founding of Kuwasan Shrine ==== Regarding the founding of Kuwayama Shrine, there is a legend in the "Yada no Saiki" written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}). . According to this, in ancient times, Izumo Okami and Yagami colluded with Mt. Kurogara to develop the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which was a mud lake. He then rides an oak boat and cuts open the Uketa Gorge (currently the mouth of {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}), and succeeds in draining the water in the direction of {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}, thus creating a vast plain. Izumo Okami was enshrined at the foot of Mt. Amaoka and was revered as Kuwayama Daimyojin. Additionally, this god did not attend the October (Kannazuki) meeting in Izumo Province, but the eight gods in the county met at Kuwayama Shrine . It is also said that the name of the shrine was given to Kuwayama because the hoes used for excavation were piled up there. (Also see "{{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin #Lake Lore|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}"). ==== Hachimangu Shrine's solicitation ==== [[File:Kuwayama-jinja koike.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|A small pond between Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine}}{{small|It is said that it was established so that the two shrines would not fight each other. }}]] Regarding the parallel Hachiman Shrine, it is said in "Yada no Saiki" that it descended to Mt. Menfuri (Mt. Amaoka) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). Since then, it has been enshrined as Shin Hachimangu Shrine next to Kuwayamagu Shrine, but there were thunderstorms and fights between pigeons and rabbits. The villagers thought this was a discord between the two gods, and it is said that the conflict was resolved by moving Hachimangu Shrine to Sugitani . Although it is unclear whether this is related to the legend, Kuwayama Shrine currently uses a rabbit, and Hachiman Shrine uses a pigeon. Even after both shrines were moved to their current location in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), a small pond was built between the two shrines to prevent conflicts, and the pond still exists today. === Outline === According to the shrine's legend, Kuwazangu Shrine was founded in the 2nd year of Wado ({{ill|lt=709|Q23514|WD=Q23514}}), and was originally located in Iou Valley, behind Mt. There are small shrines dotted all the way to the top of Mt. Menduri, and at the top of the mountain there is a shadow stone that is said to have descended from Hachimangu Shrine . There are many ancient tombs in the surrounding area, and there is also a theory that there was a kokuga or a county seat based on the legend that a {{ill|lt=tunkura|Q11465761|WD=Q11465761}} was established in the Miyake area, suggesting that Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, it is written as ``{{ill|lt=Tamba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}{{ill|lt=Kuwata District|Q11537600|WD=Q11537600}} Kuwayama Shrine,'' and it is listed as {{ill||lt=Shikiuchi Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It is said that the physician {{ill|lt=Yasuyori Tamba|Q1466794|WD=Q1466794}}, who was born in Iōdani, believed in Kuwayama Shrine, and the name ``Iōdani'' also came from Yasuyori, who was well versed in medicine.The place name ``Yada'' is related to Kuwayama Shrine, and there are 8 shrines: Otabisho, Rakuta, Yuuda, Hanada, Yokata, Sumoda, Babada, Zoyoda, and Hoshada. The shrine was called ``Hatta'' because it had , and later changed to ``Yada'' when {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yorimasa|Q164948|WD=Q164948}} received the shrine. . It is also said that festivals such as the mikoshi festival, eight-day festival, niwa kagura, horse racing, sumo wrestling, sarugaku, etc. have been held since ancient times. name=chimei/> invaded Tanba in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}4 ({{ill|lt=1576|Q6700|WD=Q6700}}) nk|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} abolished those festivals, and Daichi-in (currently an abandoned temple) was built as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}. Eight Kanda were also requisitioned . {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), {{ill|lt=Kameyama Castle (Tamba Province)|Q1037429|WD=Q1037429|lt=Kameyama Castle}} The lord {{ill|lt=Mori Okanabe|Q11473887|WD=Q11473887}} built a new shrine building at the current location and moved it, and also donated the shrine land . In {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}16 ({{ill|lt=1639|Q6897|WD=Q6897}}), the domain of the shrine was donated by Sadafusa Suganuma, the lord of the domain .In {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 9th year ({{ill|lt=1681|Q7645|WD=Q7645}}), Morichika Sugihara wrote ``Festival Chukoki'' and revived the festival . In the same year, a mikoshi was donated by the castle lord {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then the annual festival has become popular as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kuchitamba. {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in 1998, and was promoted to {{ill||lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}3. === Precincts === [[File:Kuwayama-jinja kuwayamagū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Kuwayamamiya}}]] [[File:Kuwayama-jinja hachimangū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Hachimangu Shrine}}]] The main shrine, Kuwayamagu Shrine, and Hachimangu Shrine are located within the same fence, with Kuwayamagu Shrine on the left and Hachimangu Shrine on the right. Both shrines have the same format and size, and were built in the same year as {{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}}11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943}}) of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}. Kuwayama Shrine's {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} exists since {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}}3 ({{ill|lt=1462|Q6546|WD=Q6546}}). The style is Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} and has {{ill||lt=staggered gables|UNKNOWN}}. A one-room {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293}} shrine is attached to the front, and the roof is {{ill||lt=persimmon thatched|UNKNOWN}}. Both shrines are registered as Kyoto Prefecture's registered cultural properties. [http://spogaku.pref.kyoto.lg.jp/asset/1280.html Kuwayama Shrine Precinct Shrine Hachiman Shrine Main Hall] (Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Based on the legend that Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine were not on good terms, a small pond was built between the two shrines. File:Kuwayama-jinja haiden-2.jpg|Handiden (shared with Ninomiya) File:Kuwayama-jinja torii-2.jpg|Torii in the precincts File:Kuwayama-jinja teien.jpg|Garden === Setsusha === '''Precinct shrine''' * Kanayama Shrine * Kashifune Shrine - Enshrines the same deity as {{ill|lt=Kashifune Shrine|Q11543587|WD=Q11543587}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Takatsuki City|Q335423|WD=Q335423}}. * Takagi Shrine * Hiyoshi Shrine * Kumano Shrine * Inari Hipo Shrine * Atago Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime}}. It is enshrined on a small island in the pond. * Hyakudayu Shrine - Deities enshrined: Toyowama Tokami, Kushiwama Tokami. is located at the entrance to the shrine grounds and is said to be the first shrine you should visit before visiting the head office. '''{{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}''' * Katahara Shrine (Yokomachi, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|39.21|N|135|34|51.70|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Katahara Shrine (Otabisho)}}) *: Enshrined in the ruins of a mansion south of Kameyama Castle in the center of Kameoka. Katahara Shrine is a shrine dedicated to the first lord of {{ill|lt=Tamba Kameyama Clan|Q10854981|WD=Q10854981|lt=Kameyama Clan}}, {{ill|lt=Nobuaki Matsudaira|Q8515964|WD=Q8515964}}, and was founded in 1898. Katahara Shrine] (Tamba Shrine [Personal Site]). . During the annual festival (Kameoka Festival), a mikoshi (portable shrine) is passed to the shrine as a traveling place.* Former Otabisho (Ichi-no-Torii) (Nakayada-cho, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|09.39|N|135|34|55.48|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Ichi-no-Torii (Old Otabisho)}}) *: This is where the Otabisho used to be, and currently there is a stone torii (Ichi-no-Torii) built by {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}} in the first year of {{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081}} ({{ill|lt=1652|Q6962|WD=Q6962}}). === Festival === === Annual festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150}}) * {{ill||lt=Setsubun Festival|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315}}) * Flower Festival ({{ill|lt=April 15th|Q2497|WD=Q2497}}) * Eight Day Festival ({{ill|lt=May 8th|Q2555|WD=Q2555}}) * Minazuki Festival ({{ill|lt=July 2nd|Q2697|WD=Q2697}}) * Annual Festival (Autumn Festival, {{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}) ({{ill|lt=October 1st|Q2913|WD=Q2913}}-{{ill|lt=October 26th|Q2953|WD=Q2953|lt=26th}}) ** {{ill|lt=Shinko Festival|Q123907414|WD=Q123907414}} ({{ill|lt=October 20th|Q2959|WD=Q2959}}) ** Yoyoyama ({{ill|lt=October 23rd|Q2958|WD=Q2958}}) ** Yoimiya ({{ill|lt=October 24th|Q2955|WD=Q2955}}) ** Main Festival ({{ill|lt=October 25th|Q2954|WD=Q2954}}) * Fire Festival ({{ill|lt=December 3rd|Q2298|WD=Q2298}}) === Kameoka Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} held in October is called ``''{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'''. In ancient times, {{ill|lt=September 25th (lunar calendar)|Q840119|WD=Q840119|lt=September 25th}} was the Shinko Festival, and {{ill|lt=September 30th (lunar calendar)|Q840189|WD=Q840189|lt=30th}} was the Kanko Festival. It is said that the festival at Kuwayama Shrine was abolished by {{ill|lt=Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320|WD=Q313320}} who invaded Tamba, but {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}}In 1681|Q7645}}, a mikoshi was donated by the lord of Kameyama Castle, {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then, the annual festival has been known as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kouchi-Tamba, and based on tradition, oak boats have also been paraded around the festival [http://www.k-yamahoko.com/history.html History of Yamahoko Events] ([http://www.k-yamahoko.com/index.html Kameoka Festival Homepage]) . At the festival, 11 floats are paraded like the {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}} at {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q692714|WD=Q692714}} in Kyoto, and mikoshi from both Kuwayama and Hachimangu Shrines are carried to Katahara Shrine, the Otabisho. === Cultural assets === === Kyoto prefecture registered cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Kuwayama Shrine and the Hachiman Shrine main hall (structure) - Registered on April 15, 1983 (Showa 58). * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Registered on March 19, 2004. === Kyoto Prefecture Cultural Properties Environmental Conservation District === * Kuwayama Shrine Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District - Decided on May 15, 1985. === Cultural property designated by Kameoka City === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Designated on August 3, 1998. === Others === * Kameoka's top 100 natural attractions: Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada-cho Kamigakiuchi 22 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway|Q502125|WD=Q502125}} (JR West) {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011}} {{ill|lt=Kameoka Station|Q1043696|WD=Q1043696}} **Walk: about 30 minutes ** Bus: Take the Kameoka City Community Bus (East Course) and get off at the "Kuwayama Jinja-mae" bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Nasu Yoichido === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === {{small|(References used in writing the article)}} '''Historical materials''' * “Tanba Province Kuwata District Yadano Shrine” *: Selected by Morichika Sugihara of Kameyama Gocho ({{ill|lt=Machi Toshiyori|Q11578236|WD=Q11578236}}) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}). Mt. Mendori (Mt. Amaoka) is in the background. }}]] '''Kuwayama Shrine'' (Kuwayama Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada Town. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}}. It is also commonly known as ``Yada-sha'' or ``Yada-miya''. === Overview === It is enshrined at the eastern foot of Mt. Menkoyama in the southern part of {{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}. Many ancient ruins remain in the surrounding area, including Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which is said to have been a lake in ancient times, the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Oomi Takamikoto (Ōkuninushi)}} This shrine is based on the legend that, as part of the efforts to build the country, {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}} was excavated to create a basin, and the hoes used for the excavation were piled up here. Within the fence of the precincts, Hachiman Shrine is built alongside the main shrine in the same size and style, and both shrine buildings are registered as {{ill|lt=List of Kyoto Prefecture Designated and Registered Cultural Properties|Q11375201|WD=Q11375201|lt=Kyoto Prefecture Registered Cultural Properties}}. Many autumn leaves are planted in the precincts, and it is also a famous spot for autumn leaves. In addition, the annual festival ``{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'' is a traditional festival in Kameoka City, where people carry the gods to Kameoka City. === Enshrined God === * Kuwayama Shrine: '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōmi Takamikoto}}''' (Onamuchi no Mikoto) *: Okuninushi is the same as {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} and is an Izumo god. It is said to be the main deity that has been enshrined since its founding. * Hachiman Shrine: '''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honda Beszon|UNKNOWN}}''' (Hondawake no Mikoto) *: Honda Besson is the same as {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, and {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}. It is said to be a guest deity that has been enshrined since the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). The divine crest of Kuwayama Shrine is a rabbit, in honor of Okuninushi no Mikoto, and that of Hachiman Shrine is a dove, as is customary. === History === === Legend === [[File:Hozukyō (Kameoka).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Entrance to the traditional site of {{ill|lt=Hozukyo|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Ukeda Shrine|Q11631810|WD=Q11631810}} on the left bank, {{ill|lt=Kuwata Shrine #Kuwata Shrine (Yamamoto, Shinomachi, Kameoka City)|Q11537594|WD=Q11537594|lt=Kuwata Shrine}} is enshrined there. }}]] ==== Founding of Kuwasan Shrine ==== Regarding the founding of Kuwayama Shrine, there is a legend in the "Yada no Saiki" written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}). . According to this, in ancient times, Izumo Okami and Yagami colluded with Mt. Kurogara to develop the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which was a mud lake. He then rides an oak boat and cuts open the Uketa Gorge (currently the mouth of {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}), and succeeds in draining the water in the direction of {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}, thus creating a vast plain. Izumo Okami was enshrined at the foot of Mt. Amaoka and was revered as Kuwayama Daimyojin. Additionally, this god did not attend the October (Kannazuki) meeting in Izumo Province, but the eight gods in the county met at Kuwayama Shrine . It is also said that the name of the shrine was given to Kuwayama because the hoes used for excavation were piled up there. (Also see "{{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin #Lake Lore|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}"). ==== Hachimangu Shrine's solicitation ==== [[File:Kuwayama-jinja koike.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|A small pond between Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine}}{{small|It is said that it was established so that the two shrines would not fight each other. }}]] Regarding the parallel Hachiman Shrine, it is said in "Yada no Saiki" that it descended to Mt. Menfuri (Mt. Amaoka) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). Since then, it has been enshrined as Shin Hachimangu Shrine next to Kuwayamagu Shrine, but there were thunderstorms and fights between pigeons and rabbits. The villagers thought this was a discord between the two gods, and it is said that the conflict was resolved by moving Hachimangu Shrine to Sugitani . Although it is unclear whether this is related to the legend, Kuwayama Shrine currently uses a rabbit, and Hachiman Shrine uses a pigeon. Even after both shrines were moved to their current location in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), a small pond was built between the two shrines to prevent conflicts, and the pond still exists today. === Outline === According to the shrine's legend, Kuwazangu Shrine was founded in the 2nd year of Wado ({{ill|lt=709|Q23514|WD=Q23514}}), and was originally located in Iou Valley, behind Mt. There are small shrines dotted all the way to the top of Mt. Menduri, and at the top of the mountain there is a shadow stone that is said to have descended from Hachimangu Shrine . There are many ancient tombs in the surrounding area, and there is also a theory that there was a kokuga or a county seat based on the legend that a {{ill|lt=tunkura|Q11465761|WD=Q11465761}} was established in the Miyake area, suggesting that Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, it is written as ``{{ill|lt=Tamba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}{{ill|lt=Kuwata District|Q11537600|WD=Q11537600}} Kuwayama Shrine,'' and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It is said that the physician {{ill|lt=Yasuyori Tamba|Q1466794|WD=Q1466794}}, who was born in Iōdani, believed in Kuwayama Shrine, and the name ``Iōdani'' also came from Yasuyori, who was well versed in medicine.The place name ``Yada'' is related to Kuwayama Shrine, and there are 8 shrines: Otabisho, Rakuta, Yuuda, Hanada, Yokata, Sumoda, Babada, Zoyoda, and Hoshada. The shrine was called ``Hatta'' because it had , and later changed to ``Yada'' when {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yorimasa|Q164948|WD=Q164948}} received the shrine. . It is also said that festivals such as the mikoshi festival, eight-day festival, niwa kagura, horse racing, sumo wrestling, sarugaku, etc. have been held since ancient times. name=chimei/> invaded Tanba in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}4 ({{ill|lt=1576|Q6700|WD=Q6700}}) nk|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} abolished those festivals, and Daichi-in (currently an abandoned temple) was built as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}. Eight Kanda were also requisitioned . {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), {{ill|lt=Kameyama Castle (Tamba Province)|Q1037429|WD=Q1037429|lt=Kameyama Castle}} The lord {{ill|lt=Mori Okanabe|Q11473887|WD=Q11473887}} built a new shrine building at the current location and moved it, and also donated the shrine land . In {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}16 ({{ill|lt=1639|Q6897|WD=Q6897}}), the domain of the shrine was donated by Sadafusa Suganuma, the lord of the domain .In {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 9th year ({{ill|lt=1681|Q7645|WD=Q7645}}), Morichika Sugihara wrote ``Festival Chukoki'' and revived the festival . In the same year, a mikoshi was donated by the castle lord {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then the annual festival has become popular as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kuchitamba. {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in 1998, and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}3. === Precincts === [[File:Kuwayama-jinja kuwayamagū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Kuwayamamiya}}]] [[File:Kuwayama-jinja hachimangū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Hachimangu Shrine}}]] The main shrine, Kuwayamagu Shrine, and Hachimangu Shrine are located within the same fence, with Kuwayamagu Shrine on the left and Hachimangu Shrine on the right. Both shrines have the same format and size, and were built in the same year as {{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}}11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943}}) of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}. Kuwayama Shrine's {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} exists since {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}}3 ({{ill|lt=1462|Q6546|WD=Q6546}}). The style is Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} and has {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=staggered gables|UNKNOWN}}. A one-room {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293}} shrine is attached to the front, and the roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=persimmon thatched|UNKNOWN}}. Both shrines are registered as Kyoto Prefecture's registered cultural properties. [http://spogaku.pref.kyoto.lg.jp/asset/1280.html Kuwayama Shrine Precinct Shrine Hachiman Shrine Main Hall] (Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Based on the legend that Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine were not on good terms, a small pond was built between the two shrines. File:Kuwayama-jinja haiden-2.jpg|Handiden (shared with Ninomiya) File:Kuwayama-jinja torii-2.jpg|Torii in the precincts File:Kuwayama-jinja teien.jpg|Garden === Setsusha === '''Precinct shrine''' * Kanayama Shrine * Kashifune Shrine - Enshrines the same deity as {{ill|lt=Kashifune Shrine|Q11543587|WD=Q11543587}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Takatsuki City|Q335423|WD=Q335423}}. * Takagi Shrine * Hiyoshi Shrine * Kumano Shrine * Inari Hipo Shrine * Atago Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime}}. It is enshrined on a small island in the pond. * Hyakudayu Shrine - Deities enshrined: Toyowama Tokami, Kushiwama Tokami. is located at the entrance to the shrine grounds and is said to be the first shrine you should visit before visiting the head office. '''{{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}''' * Katahara Shrine (Yokomachi, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|39.21|N|135|34|51.70|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Katahara Shrine (Otabisho)}}) *: Enshrined in the ruins of a mansion south of Kameyama Castle in the center of Kameoka. Katahara Shrine is a shrine dedicated to the first lord of {{ill|lt=Tamba Kameyama Clan|Q10854981|WD=Q10854981|lt=Kameyama Clan}}, {{ill|lt=Nobuaki Matsudaira|Q8515964|WD=Q8515964}}, and was founded in 1898. Katahara Shrine] (Tamba Shrine [Personal Site]). . During the annual festival (Kameoka Festival), a mikoshi (portable shrine) is passed to the shrine as a traveling place.* Former Otabisho (Ichi-no-Torii) (Nakayada-cho, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|09.39|N|135|34|55.48|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Ichi-no-Torii (Old Otabisho)}}) *: This is where the Otabisho used to be, and currently there is a stone torii (Ichi-no-Torii) built by {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}} in the first year of {{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081}} ({{ill|lt=1652|Q6962|WD=Q6962}}). === Festival === === Annual festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Setsubun Festival|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315}}) * Flower Festival ({{ill|lt=April 15th|Q2497|WD=Q2497}}) * Eight Day Festival ({{ill|lt=May 8th|Q2555|WD=Q2555}}) * Minazuki Festival ({{ill|lt=July 2nd|Q2697|WD=Q2697}}) * Annual Festival (Autumn Festival, {{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}) ({{ill|lt=October 1st|Q2913|WD=Q2913}}-{{ill|lt=October 26th|Q2953|WD=Q2953|lt=26th}}) ** {{ill|lt=Shinko Festival|Q123907414|WD=Q123907414}} ({{ill|lt=October 20th|Q2959|WD=Q2959}}) ** Yoyoyama ({{ill|lt=October 23rd|Q2958|WD=Q2958}}) ** Yoimiya ({{ill|lt=October 24th|Q2955|WD=Q2955}}) ** Main Festival ({{ill|lt=October 25th|Q2954|WD=Q2954}}) * Fire Festival ({{ill|lt=December 3rd|Q2298|WD=Q2298}}) === Kameoka Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} held in October is called ``''{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'''. In ancient times, {{ill|lt=September 25th (lunar calendar)|Q840119|WD=Q840119|lt=September 25th}} was the Shinko Festival, and {{ill|lt=September 30th (lunar calendar)|Q840189|WD=Q840189|lt=30th}} was the Kanko Festival. It is said that the festival at Kuwayama Shrine was abolished by {{ill|lt=Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320|WD=Q313320}} who invaded Tamba, but {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}}In 1681|Q7645}}, a mikoshi was donated by the lord of Kameyama Castle, {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then, the annual festival has been known as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kouchi-Tamba, and based on tradition, oak boats have also been paraded around the festival [http://www.k-yamahoko.com/history.html History of Yamahoko Events] ([http://www.k-yamahoko.com/index.html Kameoka Festival Homepage]) . At the festival, 11 floats are paraded like the {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}} at {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q692714|WD=Q692714}} in Kyoto, and mikoshi from both Kuwayama and Hachimangu Shrines are carried to Katahara Shrine, the Otabisho. === Cultural assets === === Kyoto prefecture registered cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Kuwayama Shrine and the Hachiman Shrine main hall (structure) - Registered on April 15, 1983 (Showa 58). * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Registered on March 19, 2004. === Kyoto Prefecture Cultural Properties Environmental Conservation District === * Kuwayama Shrine Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District - Decided on May 15, 1985. === Cultural property designated by Kameoka City === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Designated on August 3, 1998. === Others === * Kameoka's top 100 natural attractions: Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada-cho Kamigakiuchi 22 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway|Q502125|WD=Q502125}} (JR West) {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011}} {{ill|lt=Kameoka Station|Q1043696|WD=Q1043696}} **Walk: about 30 minutes ** Bus: Take the Kameoka City Community Bus (East Course) and get off at the "Kuwayama Jinja-mae" bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Nasu Yoichido === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === {{small|(References used in writing the article)}} '''Historical materials''' * “Tanba Province Kuwata District Yadano Shrine” *: Selected by Morichika Sugihara of Kameyama Gocho ({{ill|lt=Machi Toshiyori|Q11578236|WD=Q11578236}}) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}). Mt. Mendori (Mt. Amaoka) is in the background. }}]] '''Kuwayama Shrine'' (Kuwayama Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada Town. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old shrine rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also commonly known as ``Yada-sha'' or ``Yada-miya''. === Overview === It is enshrined at the eastern foot of Mt. Menkoyama in the southern part of {{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}. Many ancient ruins remain in the surrounding area, including Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which is said to have been a lake in ancient times, the enshrined deity {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Oomi Takamikoto (Ōkuninushi)}} This shrine is based on the legend that, as part of the efforts to build the country, {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}} was excavated to create a basin, and the hoes used for the excavation were piled up here. Within the fence of the precincts, Hachiman Shrine is built alongside the main shrine in the same size and style, and both shrine buildings are registered as {{ill|lt=List of Kyoto Prefecture Designated and Registered Cultural Properties|Q11375201|WD=Q11375201|lt=Kyoto Prefecture Registered Cultural Properties}}. Many autumn leaves are planted in the precincts, and it is also a famous spot for autumn leaves. In addition, the annual festival ``{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'' is a traditional festival in Kameoka City, where people carry the gods to Kameoka City. === Enshrined God === * Kuwayama Shrine: '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōmi Takamikoto}}''' (Onamuchi no Mikoto) *: Okuninushi is the same as {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} and is an Izumo god. It is said to be the main deity that has been enshrined since its founding. * Hachiman Shrine: '''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honda Beszon|1=UNKNOWN}}''' (Hondawake no Mikoto) *: Honda Besson is the same as {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}}, and {{ill|lt=Hachimanjin|Q261637|WD=Q261637}}. It is said to be a guest deity that has been enshrined since the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). The divine crest of Kuwayama Shrine is a rabbit, in honor of Okuninushi no Mikoto, and that of Hachiman Shrine is a dove, as is customary. === History === === Legend === [[File:Hozukyō (Kameoka).JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Entrance to the traditional site of {{ill|lt=Hozukyo|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Ukeda Shrine|Q11631810|WD=Q11631810}} on the left bank, {{ill|lt=Kuwata Shrine #Kuwata Shrine (Yamamoto, Shinomachi, Kameoka City)|Q11537594|WD=Q11537594|lt=Kuwata Shrine}} is enshrined there. }}]] ==== Founding of Kuwasan Shrine ==== Regarding the founding of Kuwayama Shrine, there is a legend in the "Yada no Saiki" written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}). . According to this, in ancient times, Izumo Okami and Yagami colluded with Mt. Kurogara to develop the {{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}, which was a mud lake. He then rides an oak boat and cuts open the Uketa Gorge (currently the mouth of {{ill|lt=Hozu Gorge|Q10887680|WD=Q10887680}}), and succeeds in draining the water in the direction of {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}, thus creating a vast plain. Izumo Okami was enshrined at the foot of Mt. Amaoka and was revered as Kuwayama Daimyojin. Additionally, this god did not attend the October (Kannazuki) meeting in Izumo Province, but the eight gods in the county met at Kuwayama Shrine . It is also said that the name of the shrine was given to Kuwayama because the hoes used for excavation were piled up there. (Also see "{{ill|lt=Kameoka Basin #Lake Lore|Q11181997|WD=Q11181997}}"). ==== Hachimangu Shrine's solicitation ==== [[File:Kuwayama-jinja koike.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|A small pond between Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine}}{{small|It is said that it was established so that the two shrines would not fight each other. }}]] Regarding the parallel Hachiman Shrine, it is said in "Yada no Saiki" that it descended to Mt. Menfuri (Mt. Amaoka) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Eiman|Q1193868|WD=Q1193868}} ({{ill|lt=1165|Q19676|WD=Q19676}}). Since then, it has been enshrined as Shin Hachimangu Shrine next to Kuwayamagu Shrine, but there were thunderstorms and fights between pigeons and rabbits. The villagers thought this was a discord between the two gods, and it is said that the conflict was resolved by moving Hachimangu Shrine to Sugitani . Although it is unclear whether this is related to the legend, Kuwayama Shrine currently uses a rabbit, and Hachiman Shrine uses a pigeon. Even after both shrines were moved to their current location in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), a small pond was built between the two shrines to prevent conflicts, and the pond still exists today. === Outline === According to the shrine's legend, Kuwazangu Shrine was founded in the 2nd year of Wado ({{ill|lt=709|Q23514|WD=Q23514}}), and was originally located in Iou Valley, behind Mt. There are small shrines dotted all the way to the top of Mt. Menduri, and at the top of the mountain there is a shadow stone that is said to have descended from Hachimangu Shrine . There are many ancient tombs in the surrounding area, and there is also a theory that there was a kokuga or a county seat based on the legend that a {{ill|lt=tunkura|Q11465761|WD=Q11465761}} was established in the Miyake area, suggesting that Mt. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} from the middle of the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, it is written as ``{{ill|lt=Tamba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}{{ill|lt=Kuwata District|Q11537600|WD=Q11537600}} Kuwayama Shrine,'' and it is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is said that the physician {{ill|lt=Yasuyori Tamba|Q1466794|WD=Q1466794}}, who was born in Iōdani, believed in Kuwayama Shrine, and the name ``Iōdani'' also came from Yasuyori, who was well versed in medicine.The place name ``Yada'' is related to Kuwayama Shrine, and there are 8 shrines: Otabisho, Rakuta, Yuuda, Hanada, Yokata, Sumoda, Babada, Zoyoda, and Hoshada. The shrine was called ``Hatta'' because it had , and later changed to ``Yada'' when {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yorimasa|Q164948|WD=Q164948}} received the shrine. . It is also said that festivals such as the mikoshi festival, eight-day festival, niwa kagura, horse racing, sumo wrestling, sarugaku, etc. have been held since ancient times. name=chimei/> invaded Tanba in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}4 ({{ill|lt=1576|Q6700|WD=Q6700}}) nk|Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320}} abolished those festivals, and Daichi-in (currently an abandoned temple) was built as {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}. Eight Kanda were also requisitioned . {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|WD=Q6762}}), {{ill|lt=Kameyama Castle (Tamba Province)|Q1037429|WD=Q1037429|lt=Kameyama Castle}} The lord {{ill|lt=Mori Okanabe|Q11473887|WD=Q11473887}} built a new shrine building at the current location and moved it, and also donated the shrine land . In {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}16 ({{ill|lt=1639|Q6897|WD=Q6897}}), the domain of the shrine was donated by Sadafusa Suganuma, the lord of the domain .In {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 9th year ({{ill|lt=1681|Q7645|WD=Q7645}}), Morichika Sugihara wrote ``Festival Chukoki'' and revived the festival . In the same year, a mikoshi was donated by the castle lord {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then the annual festival has become popular as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kuchitamba. {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in 1998, and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Fusha|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}3. === Precincts === [[File:Kuwayama-jinja kuwayamagū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Kuwayamamiya}}]] [[File:Kuwayama-jinja hachimangū-2.jpg|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Hachimangu Shrine}}]] The main shrine, Kuwayamagu Shrine, and Hachimangu Shrine are located within the same fence, with Kuwayamagu Shrine on the left and Hachimangu Shrine on the right. Both shrines have the same format and size, and were built in the same year as {{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}}11 ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943}}) of the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}. Kuwayama Shrine's {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} exists since {{ill|lt=Kansho|Q1143928|WD=Q1143928}}3 ({{ill|lt=1462|Q6546|WD=Q6546}}). The style is Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} and has {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=staggered gables|1=UNKNOWN}}. A one-room {{ill|lt=kara gable|Q977293|WD=Q977293}} shrine is attached to the front, and the roof is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=persimmon thatched|1=UNKNOWN}}. Both shrines are registered as Kyoto Prefecture's registered cultural properties. [http://spogaku.pref.kyoto.lg.jp/asset/1280.html Kuwayama Shrine Precinct Shrine Hachiman Shrine Main Hall] (Kyoto Prefecture homepage). .Based on the legend that Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine were not on good terms, a small pond was built between the two shrines. File:Kuwayama-jinja haiden-2.jpg|Handiden (shared with Ninomiya) File:Kuwayama-jinja torii-2.jpg|Torii in the precincts File:Kuwayama-jinja teien.jpg|Garden === Setsusha === '''Precinct shrine''' * Kanayama Shrine * Kashifune Shrine - Enshrines the same deity as {{ill|lt=Kashifune Shrine|Q11543587|WD=Q11543587}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Takatsuki City|Q335423|WD=Q335423}}. * Takagi Shrine * Hiyoshi Shrine * Kumano Shrine * Inari Hipo Shrine * Atago Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime}}. It is enshrined on a small island in the pond. * Hyakudayu Shrine - Deities enshrined: Toyowama Tokami, Kushiwama Tokami. is located at the entrance to the shrine grounds and is said to be the first shrine you should visit before visiting the head office. '''{{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}''' * Katahara Shrine (Yokomachi, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|39.21|N|135|34|51.70|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Katahara Shrine (Otabisho)}}) *: Enshrined in the ruins of a mansion south of Kameyama Castle in the center of Kameoka. Katahara Shrine is a shrine dedicated to the first lord of {{ill|lt=Tamba Kameyama Clan|Q10854981|WD=Q10854981|lt=Kameyama Clan}}, {{ill|lt=Nobuaki Matsudaira|Q8515964|WD=Q8515964}}, and was founded in 1898. Katahara Shrine] (Tamba Shrine [Personal Site]). . During the annual festival (Kameoka Festival), a mikoshi (portable shrine) is passed to the shrine as a traveling place.* Former Otabisho (Ichi-no-Torii) (Nakayada-cho, Kameoka City, {{Wiki coordinates|35|00|09.39|N|135|34|55.48|E|region:JP-26|location|name=Ichi-no-Torii (Old Otabisho)}}) *: This is where the Otabisho used to be, and currently there is a stone torii (Ichi-no-Torii) built by {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}} in the first year of {{ill|lt=Joo|Q1145081|WD=Q1145081}} ({{ill|lt=1652|Q6962|WD=Q6962}}). === Festival === === Annual festival === * {{ill|lt=Saidan Festival|Q11546824|WD=Q11546824}} ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Setsubun Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315}}) * Flower Festival ({{ill|lt=April 15th|Q2497|WD=Q2497}}) * Eight Day Festival ({{ill|lt=May 8th|Q2555|WD=Q2555}}) * Minazuki Festival ({{ill|lt=July 2nd|Q2697|WD=Q2697}}) * Annual Festival (Autumn Festival, {{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}) ({{ill|lt=October 1st|Q2913|WD=Q2913}}-{{ill|lt=October 26th|Q2953|WD=Q2953|lt=26th}}) ** {{ill|lt=Shinko Festival|Q123907414|WD=Q123907414}} ({{ill|lt=October 20th|Q2959|WD=Q2959}}) ** Yoyoyama ({{ill|lt=October 23rd|Q2958|WD=Q2958}}) ** Yoimiya ({{ill|lt=October 24th|Q2955|WD=Q2955}}) ** Main Festival ({{ill|lt=October 25th|Q2954|WD=Q2954}}) * Fire Festival ({{ill|lt=December 3rd|Q2298|WD=Q2298}}) === Kameoka Festival === The {{ill|lt=regular festival|Q11385469|WD=Q11385469}} held in October is called ``''{{ill|lt=Kameoka Festival|Q11370928|WD=Q11370928}}'''. In ancient times, {{ill|lt=September 25th (lunar calendar)|Q840119|WD=Q840119|lt=September 25th}} was the Shinko Festival, and {{ill|lt=September 30th (lunar calendar)|Q840189|WD=Q840189|lt=30th}} was the Kanko Festival. It is said that the festival at Kuwayama Shrine was abolished by {{ill|lt=Akechi Mitsuhide|Q313320|WD=Q313320}} who invaded Tamba, but {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}}In 1681|Q7645}}, a mikoshi was donated by the lord of Kameyama Castle, {{ill|lt=Tadaharu Matsudaira|Q11069865|WD=Q11069865}}, and since then, the annual festival has been known as the Kameyama Festival (later Kameoka Festival), the largest festival in Kouchi-Tamba, and based on tradition, oak boats have also been paraded around the festival [http://www.k-yamahoko.com/history.html History of Yamahoko Events] ([http://www.k-yamahoko.com/index.html Kameoka Festival Homepage]) . At the festival, 11 floats are paraded like the {{ill|lt=Gion Festival|Q979873|WD=Q979873}} at {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q692714|WD=Q692714}} in Kyoto, and mikoshi from both Kuwayama and Hachimangu Shrines are carried to Katahara Shrine, the Otabisho. === Cultural assets === === Kyoto prefecture registered cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Kuwayama Shrine and the Hachiman Shrine main hall (structure) - Registered on April 15, 1983 (Showa 58). * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Registered on March 19, 2004. === Kyoto Prefecture Cultural Properties Environmental Conservation District === * Kuwayama Shrine Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District - Decided on May 15, 1985. === Cultural property designated by Kameoka City === * Intangible folk cultural assets ** Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Event - The protection organization is the Kameoka Festival Yamahoko Association. Designated on August 3, 1998. === Others === * Kameoka's top 100 natural attractions: Kuwayama Shrine and Hachiman Shrine === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka City|Q633996|WD=Q633996}}Kamiyada-cho Kamigakiuchi 22 '''Access''' * From {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway|Q502125|WD=Q502125}} (JR West) {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011}} {{ill|lt=Kameoka Station|Q1043696|WD=Q1043696}} **Walk: about 30 minutes ** Bus: Take the Kameoka City Community Bus (East Course) and get off at the "Kuwayama Jinja-mae" bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Nasu Yoichido === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === {{small|(References used in writing the article)}} '''Historical materials''' * “Tanba Province Kuwata District Yadano Shrine” *: Selected by Morichika Sugihara of Kameyama Gocho ({{ill|lt=Machi Toshiyori|Q11578236|WD=Q11578236}}) in the first year of {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} ({{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}).
